elements.sent-2411-001: Musicians delicately control their bodies to generate music.
elements.sent-2411-002: Sometimes, their motions are too subtle to be captured by the human eye.
elements.sent-2411-003: To analyze how they move to produce the music, we need to estimate precise 4D human pose (3D pose over time).
elements.sent-2411-004: However, current state-of-the-art (SoTA) visual pose estimation algorithms struggle to produce accurate monocular 4D poses because of occlusions, partial views, and human-object interactions.
elements.sent-2411-005: They are limited by the viewing angle, pixel density, and sampling rate of the cameras and fail to estimate fast and subtle movements, such as in the musical effect of vibrato.
elements.sent-2411-006: We leverage the direct causal relationship between the music produced and the human motions creating them to address these challenges.
elements.sent-2411-007: We propose \AlgName: a novel multimodal network that hierarchically estimates dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-008: High-level features are cascaded to low-level features and integrated into Bayesian updates.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our architecture is shown to produce accurate pose sequences, facilitating precise motion analysis, and outperforms SoTA.
elements.sent-2411-010: As part of this work, we collected the largest and the most diverse calibrated violin-playing dataset, including video, sound, and 3D motion capture poses.
elements.sent-2411-011: Project page: is available at https://sj-yoo.info/viopose/.
elements.sent-2411-012: Consider you are at a concert, listening to Dmitri Shostakovich's Waltz No.2 with your eyes closed.
elements.sent-2411-013: Can you visualize the violinist's motion? Now, shift your thoughts to listening to Paganini Caprice No. 24.
elements.sent-2411-014: Would you still envision a similar motion? Likely not.
elements.sent-2411-015: Our brain makes different visual estimates based on auditory input, and vice versa~\cite{mcgurkHearingLipsSeeing1976 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Sound provides cues for human motion understanding~\cite{arandjelovicLookListenLearn2017 , especially in the context of music performance~\cite{tsaySightSoundJudgment2013, lerchInterdisciplinaryReviewMusic2020, essidFusionMultimodalInformation2012,duanAudiovisualAnalysisMusic2019, godoyBodyMovementMusic2009 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: The interplay between music and human motion has captivated researchers, prompting in the last decade studies along various directions.
elements.sent-2411-018: One direction is generating related music from videos of musical performance, such as piano music from performance videos~\cite{suAudeoAudioGeneration2020 and the sounds of other instruments~\cite{ganFoleyMusicLearning2020a .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Similarly, dance videos can be the basis for music generation~\cite{zhuQuantizedGANComplex2022 , and human motion videos can be used to create rhythmic sounds.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Conversely, the transformation from audio to human motion has also been explored, for example, to synthesize novel dance motions from music~\cite{leeDancingMusic2019, marchellusM2CConciseMusic2023, puMusicdrivenDanceRegeneration2022 , or to estimate human skeleton motion from piano~\cite{shlizermanAudioBodyDynamics2018 and violin music~\cite{kaoTemporallyGuidedMusictoBodyMovement2020, shresthaAIMusicGuruMusicAssisted2022 .}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/VioPose_Teaser.png} \caption{A 4D pose estimation in a violin performance, which features fine grained motion (left hand vibrato, $\approx$ 10 mm perturbation) and large motions (right hand bowing motion). \AlgName \ successfully estimates both motions, while other approaches fail. See Figs.~\ref{fig:vibrato} and ~\ref{fig:trajectory} for detailed real experimental results.} \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-021: In the literature on human pose estimation, state-of-the-art methods estimate human pose directly from images using different approaches such as RNN architectures~\cite{hossainExploitingTemporalInformation2018, leePropagatingLSTM3D2018 , CNN architectures~\cite{pavlakosOrdinalDepthSupervision2018, pavllo3DHumanPose2019 attention mechanisms~\cite{liExploitingTemporalContexts2022, liTokenPoseLearningKeypoint2021,zhaoPoseFormerV2ExploringFrequency2023, tang3DHumanPose2023 , and diffusion architectures~\cite{choiDiffuPoseMonocular3D2022, gongDiffPoseMoreReliable2023 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Although these methods, based on local image features, ensure high accuracy in many scenarios, they are often not robust to occlusion.
elements.sent-2411-023: To address this robustness issue, other state-of-the-art methods use physical constraints, e.g. skeleton aware architectures based on graph neural networks~\cite{ciOptimizingNetworkStructure2019, caiExploitingSpatialTemporalRelationships2019 , algorithms estimating standardized body parameters~\cite{kanazawaEndtoEndRecoveryHuman2018, liHybrIKHybridAnalyticalNeural2021 , or actively change the camera to get better view~\cite{ActPose .}}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.90\linewidth]{fig/architecture.pdf} \caption{ Overview of our proposed \AlgName, a multimodal hierarchical 4D pose estimation pipeline, which receives 2D keypoints computed by an off-the-shelf algorithm and corresponding music-playing audio. Black and purple solid circles represent concatenation and averaging operations, respectively. The architecture consists of three main components: the single modality encoder (green and orange box, $\S$~\ref{sec:single_modality}), the hierarchy module (blue box, $\S$~\ref{sec:hierarchy}), and the mixing module (purple box, $\S$~\ref{sec:mixing}). } \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-024: Human pose estimation research has rarely utilized auditory information, relying predominantly on visual data.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, using only visual information to analyze musical performances has limitations.
elements.sent-2411-026: This is because of the challenges in processing music performance videos.
elements.sent-2411-027: First, human movements can be too subtle (e.g., in vibrato, a subtle but quick left-hand motion that fluctuates the pitch slightly to decorate and enhance the musical expression.), which are often ignored because of minor impact on the Mean Per Joint Position Error (MPJPE) (see Fig.~\ref{fig:teaser ).}
elements.sent-2411-028: Second, music performance also contains fast and large motion (e.g., bowing motion).
elements.sent-2411-029: Lastly, there are occlusions of the players by their instruments.
elements.sent-2411-030: Thus we should leverage sound information to estimate accurate human poses.
elements.sent-2411-031: However, there is a lack of datasets with music and 4D pose.
elements.sent-2411-032: To address these challenges, we introduce a new dataset and a novel architecture called \DataName~and \AlgName.
elements.sent-2411-033: Our multi-modal dataset (Mocap, vision, and audio) has 639 videos of 12 violin players of different gender, ages, size, and skill levels playing a variety of exercises as well as freestyle pieces.
elements.sent-2411-034: Our algorithm, audiovisual Violin performance 4D Pose estimation, receives audiovisual inputs\textemdash off-the-shelf 2D pose estimates and correlated raw audio\textemdash and estimates 3D pose as output, being trained supervised by ground truth pose collected from motion capture.
elements.sent-2411-035: The hierarchical architecture cascades the high-level information to the lower-level layer and fuses in the way of Bayesian updates.
elements.sent-2411-036: These cross-modal inference modules allow high-quality 3D pose estimation.
elements.sent-2411-037: We benchmark \AlgName \ on our dataset \DataName \ against different SoTA networks.
elements.sent-2411-038: For a fair comparison, we retrained all other models with \DataName.
elements.sent-2411-039: Extensive ablation studies were conducted to show the efficacy of proposed modules in the \AlgName.
elements.sent-2411-040: In summary, our contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-041: We introduce a novel audiovisual monocular 4D pose estimation network that consists of a hierarchical structure.
elements.sent-2411-042: Recent state-of-the-art methods for 3D human pose estimation can be categorized into direct regression and physically constrained methods.
elements.sent-2411-043: Direct regression methods rely on image features to directly locate joint positions~\cite{weiConvolutionalPoseMachines2016, pavlakosOrdinalDepthSupervision2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Recent literature uses well-trained 2D keypoint estimation architectures as input and uplifts them to 3D.
elements.sent-2411-045: For instance, ~\citet{pavllo3DHumanPose2019 propose a CNN architecture that exploits the temporal relationship of the input 2D keypoints.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Similarly, the Transformer architecture has been used to extract spatial relationships between joints and temporal consistency to uplift from 2D to 3D~\cite{zheng3DHumanPose2021,yangUshapedSpatialTemporal2022, zhaoPoseFormerV2ExploringFrequency2023, tang3DHumanPose2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Although these approaches promise high accuracy, they often suffer from occlusion and estimate implausible human poses.
elements.sent-2411-048: Methods that explicitly consider physical constraints in the architectures have used standardized human body information~\cite{kanazawaEndtoEndRecoveryHuman2018 , kinematics~\cite{chenAnatomyAware3DHuman2022, xuDeepKinematicsAnalysis2020 , and physics-based optimization methods~\cite{shimadaPhysCapPhysicallyPlausible2020, hassanResolving3DHuman2019 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: However, most methods use visual information only.
elements.sent-2411-050: As discussed in the introduction (see Section~\ref{sec:intro ), there is a need to utilize audio information in the domain of music performance analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Not only in music, sounds exhibit a strong correlation with motion, paving the way for the design of cross-modal inference architectures.
elements.sent-2411-052: For example, \citet{suPhysicsDrivenDiffusionModels2023 introduces a novel physics-driven diffusion model to estimate impact sounds from corresponding videos.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Similarly, generating a talking face from speech, utilizing the combination of audio and visual features has been highlighted~\cite{SynthesizingObamaLearning, parkSyncTalkFaceTalkingFace2022, zhouPoseControllableTalkingFace2021, LipSyncExpert .}
elements.sent-2411-054: Moreover, \citet{ginosarLearningIndividualStyles2019 addresses the speech-to-gesture generation problem using a GAN model.}
elements.sent-2411-055: However, since different from music performance, speech and gesture are correlated but not {\it causally related , cross-modality may not be justified.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Extending beyond these applications, multimodal audiovisual architectures have been developed to tackle complex challenges such as sound source separation in the cocktail party problem~\cite{zhaoSoundMotions2019, zhaoSoundPixels2018, afourasSelfsupervisedLearningAudioVisual2020, liAudioVisualEndtoEndMultiChannel2023 , action recognition~\cite{chenMMViTMultiModalVideo2022, rajasekarJointCrossAttentionModel2022, kazakosEPICFusionAudioVisualTemporal2019 , navigation~\cite{chenSoundSpacesAudioVisualNavigation2020a, ganLookListenAct2020 , and depth estimation~\cite{gaoVisualEchoesSpatialImage2020 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Most audiovisual fusion methods utilize either early or mid-feature fusion architectures.
elements.sent-2411-058: Our architecture adopts a mid-fusion strategy, but unlike other architectures, auditory information is computed as a prior for high-level posterior computation.
elements.figure-2411-003: 2\columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l|ccccc|ccc|ccc|ccc} \Xhline{1pt} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{} \multicolumn{5}{c|}{\bf{General Info}} \multicolumn{3}{c|}{\bf{Pose Info}} \multicolumn{3}{c|}{\bf{Video Info}} \multicolumn{3}{c}{\bf{Audio Info}} \bf{Dataset} \bf{Type} \bf{Interact} \bf{Fine} \bf{\# Subj} \bf{Div} \bf{3D} \bf{2D} \bf{Calib} \bf{\# Vids} \bf{Len (m)} \bf{\# Cams} \bf{\# Mics} \bf{Audio} \bf{Causal} HumanEva \cite{Sigal:IJCV:10b} M \x x{} \x x{} 4 \x x{} \check c{} \check c{} \check c{} 168 22 4 0 \x x{} \x x{} Human3.6M \cite{6682899} M \x x{} \x x{} 11 \check c{} \check c{} \check c{} \check c{} 660 298 4 0 \x x{} \x x{} GrooveNet \cite{alemi2017groovenet} D \x x{} \x x{} 11 \x x{} \check c{} \x x{} \x x{} 0 0 0 1 \check c{} \x x{} DanceNet \cite{zhuang2020music2dance} D \x x{} \x x{} 2 \x x{} \check c{} \x x{} \x x{} 0 0 0 1 \check c{} \x x{} AIST++ \cite{liAIChoreographerMusic2021} D \x x{} \x x{} 30 \x x{} \check c{} \check c{} \check c{} 1408 312 9 1 \check c{} \x x{} QUARTET \cite{Papiotis:PhdThesis2016} P \check c{} \check c{} 4 \x x{} \x x{} \x x{} \x x{} 30 29 1 6 \check c{} \check c{} TELMI \cite{volpe2017multimodal} P \check c{} \check c{} 4 \x x{} \x x{} \x x{} \x x{} 292 105 2 3 \check c{} \check c{} URMP \cite{li2018creating} P \check c{} \check c{} 22 \x x{} \x x{} \x x{} \x x{} 44 78 1 1 \check c{} \check c{} \bf{\DataName \ (Ours)} \bf{P} \check c{} \check c{} \bf{12} \check c{} \check c{} \check c{} \check c{} \bf{639} \bf{133} \bf{4} \bf{4} \check c{} \check c{} \Xhline{1pt} \end{tabular} } \caption{ Comparison of 3D human kinematic pose and audio datasets. Symbols (\check c{}) means fully satisfies, (\x x{}) does not satisfy, and ($\sim$) partially satisfies the field topic. Dataset Type include General motion (M), Dance (D), and Playing (P) instruments. Interact refers to people interacting with objects, thus making the dataset difficult for pose estimation. Fine refers to the motion velocity and complexity, where slow-moving and large change is easier to observe. In contrast, fast motion and small movements are much more challenging due to motion blur and pixel saturation. In Dance datasets (D) the audio is not produced by the motions and therefore does not have a Causal relationship. The Musical instrument playing (P) datasets QUARTET and TELMI contain raw motion capture markers, audio (\# Mics), and video (\# Vids), and URMP has audio and video. These datasets lack accurate 3D human kinematic and joint information and therefore 3D and 2D Pose Ground Truth, and they do not have the information on the video calibration (Calib) making it difficult to use and compare with Pose from video. Multiple subjects (\# Subj) of diverse (Div) races, genders, ages, height, body type, and skill levels are important for a reliable dataset. \DataName is the only dataset that has a fully calibrated full-body 3D and 2D human pose with synchronized audio recorded from diverse subjects from multiple viewpoints.} \label{tab:dataset_comparison}
elements.sent-2411-059: While sound plays a key role, visual cues also contain abundant information for understanding music performance~\cite{essidFusionMultimodalInformation2012, tsaySightSoundJudgment2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-060: However, analyzing music performance videos is challenging due to the subtle or rapid movements of body parts, important limbs being highly occluded or truncated by musical instruments or other people, and the involvement of multiple musicians in a single frame~\cite{duanAudiovisualAnalysisMusic2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-061: To address these challenges, several audiovisual architectures have been proposed.
elements.sent-2411-062: For example, \citet{zhangAutomaticMusicTranscription2009 propose an audiovisual fusion algorithm for violin onset detection that combines feature-level early fusion with decision-level late fusion.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Beyond audiovisual learning, \citet{gaoAutomatedViolinBowing2023a address the problem of violin bowing technique classification using an FMCW radar system.}
elements.sent-2411-064: However, the absence of high-quality audiovisual datasets hinders the development of fundamental architectures.
elements.sent-2411-065: It is important to note that estimating accurate 3D pose is fundamental to music performance analysis.
elements.sent-2411-066: Based on joint trajectories, bowing technique classification can be accomplished~\cite{dalmazzoMachineLearningApproach2018, dalmazzoBowingGesturesClassification2019 , or instant feedback for beginners can be provided~\cite{percivalEffectiveUseMultimedia2007, blancoRealTimeSoundMotion2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-067: The network receives a sequence of 2D pose estimates from an off-the-shelf algorithm (i.e. MediaPipe~\cite{lugaresiMediaPipeFrameworkBuilding2019 ) and corresponding raw audio as input, and fuses them to estimate 3D pose sequences, velocity and acceleration as the final output of architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Firstly, the network extracts audio latent vectors from audio features using 1D CNN and Transformer layers, and temporal 2D human body structure consistency latent vectors by Transformer layers ($\S$ \ref{sec:single_modality ), respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The architecture then combines computed audiovisual latent vectors through a bottleneck layer.
elements.sent-2411-070: Secondly, the combined audiovisual latent vectors are fed into the hierarchy module to learn human motion dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-071: Specifically, the high-level features are cascaded to the lower layer and combined in a way similar to Bayesian updates (e.g., $\log p(x|y) \approx \log p(y|x) + \log p(x)$).
elements.sent-2411-072: This method efficiently combines higher-level motion dynamics features with lower-level motion dynamics features ($\S$ \ref{sec:hierarchy ).}
elements.sent-2411-073: Finally, the estimated motion dynamics are integrated by bidirectional averaging ($\S$ \ref{sec:mixing ).}
elements.sent-2411-074: Figure \ref{fig:pipeline shows an overview of our proposed pipeline.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Note that in our application, the audio is directly generated by human motion.
elements.sent-2411-076: This strong causal relationship provides significant cues for estimating 4D pose, especially subtle motion, such as vibrato (see Fig.~\ref{fig:vibrato ).}
elements.sent-2411-077: Using any off-the-shelf 2D pose estimation pipeline (i.e. MediaPipe~\cite{lugaresiMediaPipeFrameworkBuilding2019 ), we extract noisy 2D poses $X_{M}\in \mathbb{R ^{f\times J \times 2}$, where $J$ is the number of joints and $f$ is the initial input video frames.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: In addition to the 2D pose, we utilize correlated audio information $X_{A \in \mathbb{R ^{F}$, where $F \gg f$.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: The goal of the task is to generate accurate 3D human poses $X_{P \in \mathbb{R ^{f\times J\times 3}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: We then tokenize the input 2d joints ($X_{P}\in \mathbb{R ^{B \times f \times D}$) through a learnable linear embedding layer followed by a learnable positional embedding, where $B$ is the batch size, $f$ is the number of input frames, and $D$ is the dimension of the latent vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Multiple transformer layers compute temporal 2D body consistency latent vector ($e_{P ^{(n)}\in \mathbb{R ^{B\times f \times D , n \in [N_P]$) as follows: \begin{equation}\eqlabel{eq:transformer} \begin{split} \hat{e}_{P}^{(n-1)} =e_{P}^{(n-1)}+\mathrm{MHA}(\mathrm{LN}(e_{P}^{(n-1)})) e_{P}^{(n)} = \hat{e}_{P}^{(n-1)} + \mathrm{FFN}(\mathrm{LN}(\hat{e}_{P}^{(n-1)})) \end{split}\end{equation} where $\mathrm{MHA}$, $\mathrm{LN}$, and $\mathrm{FFN}$ denote multi-head attention layer, layer normalization, and fully connected layer, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: This way, the model can learn the temporal consistency of 2D human body information~\cite{zheng3DHumanPose2021 .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/Dataset.png} \caption{ Our dataset was recorded from 4 different camera views (left figure) with video at $\approx$ 30 FPS and synchronized audio using smartphones. We have a total of 12 people with different gender, age, height, violin size, and body type (right figure). } \label{fig:dataset}
elements.sent-2411-084: From the raw audio, we first compute a 35-dim audio feature vector consisting of the 1-dim envelope, 20-dim MFCC, 12-dim Chroma, 1-dim one-hot peaks, and 1-dim RMS~\cite{liAIChoreographerMusic2021, marchellusM2CConciseMusic2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-085: We use a 1D CNN to encode audio features and resample $F$ to $f$ (Table.
elements.sent-2411-086: We then extract the audio latent vector $e_{A ^{(n)}\in \mathbb{R ^{B\times f\times D}$ using Eq.\eqref{eq:transformer .}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Each latent vector ($e_{A ^{(n)}$) feeds into the highest layer of the hierarchy module.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Naturally, audio contains information on human dynamics because it is generated by the dynamic interaction between humans and instruments.
elements.sent-2411-089: Therefore, we treat audio latent vectors as prior information to compute the human motion acceleration ($\S$ ~\ref{sec:hierarchy ).}
elements.sent-2411-090: Finally, the audiovisual feature, $e_{M}\in \mathbb{R ^{B\times f \times D}$, is computed by concatenation of the body structure latent vectors and audio latent vectors, followed by a bottleneck layer (block box in Fig~\ref{fig:pipeline ).}}
elements.sent-2411-091: To estimate precise 4D pose while considering both fine (see Fig. ~\ref{fig:vibrato ) and large motions (see Fig. ~\ref{fig:trajectory ) simultaneously, we devise a hierarchical architecture as shown in the blue box in Fig.~\ref{fig:pipeline .}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The hierarchy structure consists of three layers, each directly mapped to estimate motion dynamics: the highest layer to acceleration, the middle layer to velocity and the lowest layer to pose.
elements.sent-2411-093: Higher dynamics information works as prior information of lower dynamics computation.
elements.sent-2411-094: We implicitly design the log Bayesian update rules as a cascade summation structure.
elements.sent-2411-095: Each layer receives information from the upper layer and combines it with the Transformer output.
elements.sent-2411-096: Formally, we design a cascade updating structure as follows: \begin{equation}g_{i}^{(n)} = Transformer(h_{i}^{(n-1)}) \eqlabel{eq:hierarchy} h_{i}^{(n)} = Norm(g_{i}^{(n)} + h_{i+1}^{(n)}) \eqlabel{eq:sum},\end{equation} where $i\in [3], n \in [N_{H ], h_{i ^{0 =e_{M}$, $h_{4 ^{(n) =e_{A ^{(n)}$, $\text{Norm (x)$ is a batch norm, and $\text{Transformer (x)$ is using self-attention described in Eq.~\eqref{eq:transformer .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Note that $N_H$ is the number of Transformer blocks used in audio feature embedding module.
elements.sent-2411-098: In this module, we bidirectionally mix the estimated motion dynamics as shown in the purple box in Fig.~\ref{fig:pipeline .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Firstly, the architecture generates an initial estimated acceleration ($\tilde{a _I)$) from the hierarchy module and predicts velocity by integrating it.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Then, this estimate is combined with the initial estimated velocity ($\tilde{v _I$) from the hierarchy module, and a similar computation is done to pose estimation.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Secondly, we start by differentiating the estimated pose to compute the predicted velocity and then compute the final value with the previous velocity.
elements.sent-2411-102: A similar computation is done for the acceleration as well.
elements.sent-2411-103: Formally, the bidirectional mixing module is as follows: \begin{equation}\tilde{v}_{A} = \frac{1}{2} (\tilde{v}_{I} + \int \tilde{a}_I dt) \eqlabel{eq:vel_int} \hat{p} = \frac{1}{2} (\tilde{p}_{I} + \int \tilde{v}_A dt) \eqlabel{eq:pose_int} \hat{v} = \frac{1}{2} (\tilde{v}_{A} + d \hat{p}/dt) \eqlabel{eq:vel_diff} \hat{a} = \frac{1}{2} (\tilde{a}_{I} + d \hat{v}/dt) \eqlabel{eq:acc_diff}\end{equation} Note that the order of computation is Eq~\eqref{eq:vel_int to Eq~\eqref{eq:acc_diff .}}
elements.sent-2411-104: By introducing the bidirectional mixing module, we explicitly combine estimated motion dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-105: At inference time, we further combine the final estimated motion dynamics using a Kalman filter.
elements.sent-2411-106: In other works, architectures also learn velocity, but the estimation is done at the middle layers~\cite{einfaltUpliftUpsampleEfficient2023, zhangMixSTESeq2seqMixed2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-107: However, these approaches often fail to generate fine and fast motions precisely.
elements.sent-2411-108: We empirically evaluate the efficacy of the bidirectional module in Table ~\ref{tab:abl .}
elements.sent-2411-109: Our loss function consists of three losses: the final pose estimation loss $\mathcal{L _{p}$, the velocity loss $\mathcal{L _{v}$, and the acceleration loss $\mathcal{L _{a}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: The pose estimation loss $\mathcal{L _{p}$ is defined using the MPJPE (Mean Per Joint Position Error) and the velocity and acceleration losses are defined using the max-cosine similarity~\cite{hafnerDeepHierarchicalPlanning2022a , given as follows: \begin{equation}\eqlabel{eq:loss} \begin{split} \mathcal{L}_{p}(\hat{p}, p^{GT}) = \frac{1}{f} \sum_{i=1}^{f}(\sum_{j=1}^{J} || \hat{p}_{i, j}-p_{i, j}^{GT}||_{2}) \mathcal{L}_{v}(\hat{v}, v^{GT}) = \frac{1}{f} \sum_{i=1}^{f}(\sum_{j=1}^{J} (\hat{v}/V)^{\top}(v^{GT}/V) \mathcal{L}_{a}(\hat{a}, a^{GT}) = \frac{1}{f} \sum_{i=1}^{f}(\sum_{j=1}^{J} (\hat{a}/A)^{\top}(a^{GT}/A) \mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{p} + \lambda_v \mathcal{L}_{v} + \lambda_a \mathcal{L}_{a}, \end{split}\end{equation} where $V=\max (\|\hat{v}\|, \| v^{GT}\|), A=\max (\|\hat{a}\|, \| a^{GT}\|), \lambda_{v}$ and $ \lambda_{a}$ are balancing weights.}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\eqlabel{eq:loss}  L_{p}(\hat{p}, p^{GT}) = \frac{1}{f} \sum_{i=1}^{f}(\sum_{j=1}^{J} || \hat{p}_{i, j}-p_{i, j}^{GT}||_{2}) L_{v}(\hat{v}, v^{GT}) = \frac{1}{f} \sum_{i=1}^{f}(\sum_{j=1}^{J} (\hat{v}/V)^{\top}(v^{GT}/V) L_{a}(\hat{a}, a^{GT}) = \frac{1}{f} \sum_{i=1}^{f}(\sum_{j=1}^{J} (\hat{a}/A)^{\top}(a^{GT}/A) L = L_{p} + \lambda_v L_{v} + \lambda_a L_{a}, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-111: The max-cosine similarity considers both direction and magnitude, which tends to work better than the L2 loss.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{fig/trajectory.png} \caption{ Predicted right wrist trajectories (red line) and the ground truth 3D trajectories (green line) after Procrustes projection for better comparison. Each graph contains 90 frames (3 seconds). } \label{fig:trajectory}

elements.sent-2411-112: We employed MediaPipe~\cite{lugaresiMediaPipeFrameworkBuilding2019 to extract the initial noisy 2D keypoints from the input video.}
elements.sent-2411-113: The number of input frames ($f$) is 150, and the number of audio frames ($F$) is 500.
elements.sent-2411-114: We cropped the video and audio into windows of 3 seconds and 1-second hop resulting in 24,758 samples.
elements.sent-2411-115: The 1D CNN layers of the audio module have filter size $\{64, 32, 16\}$ and kernel size $\{3, 3, 3\}$ with strides $\{1, 1, 1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-116: We used three layers in the Transformer and three stacked hierarchical modules ($N_P=3$ and $N_H=3$).
elements.sent-2411-117: We used 256 hidden dimensions for the Transformer.
elements.sent-2411-118: We trained the network with the Adam optimizer for 150 epochs with a mini-batch size of 64.
elements.sent-2411-119: The learning rate is set to $1\times 10^3$ and reduced to $5\times 10^4$ and $1\times 10^4$ at 50 and 100 epochs, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-120: We divided the dataset into training, validation, and test sets by participants.
elements.sent-2411-121: The test set contains 3 participants (an advanced male adult, a novice female teenager, and a novice male child) and has $\approx$ 30 \% of the total dataset.
elements.sent-2411-122: We randomly mixed the other 70 \% data and divided it into 90 \% for the training and 10 \% for the validation.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{fig/vibrato.png} \caption{ Predicted left hand trajectories (red line) and the ground truth 3D trajectories (green line) in the vibrato movement after center alignment for better comparison. Most of the SoTAs estimate simple straight lines, but \AlgName \ is able to estimate the fine vibrato motion. Note that MHFormer looks like it is able to estimate vibrato but the movement contains high jitter estimation. We can verify this from the trajectories in Fig.~\ref{fig:trajectory}, or the MPJVE and MPJAE metrics in Table~\ref{tab:result_human3d_pose}). } \label{fig:vibrato}
elements.sent-2411-123: We collected a new violin music dataset called \DataName\ that can be used for accurate pose estimation of players.
elements.sent-2411-124: It is the largest multimodal dataset (MoCap data, 4 cameras, and 4 audio sensors) and includes 12 participants with varying levels of expertise from novice (7 participants) to advanced players (5) playing simple bowing exercises, \'etudes, and general musical pieces with simple to complex bowing techniques totaling 639 videos.
elements.sent-2411-125: In the first part of the data collection, players followed a strict protocol, and in the second half, they played varying styles and types of music.
elements.sent-2411-126: Data were recorded from four 720p resolution camera views as shown in Fig.\ref{fig:dataset that were fully calibrated and synchronized with the 12-camera Vicon 3d motion capture system (mocap).}
elements.sent-2411-127: Video data was sampled at approximately 30 frames per second (fps), audio at 44.1 kHz, and mocap at 100 Hz with 0.1mm accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-128: All participants wore mocap suits and had carefully placed retro-reflective markers on their hands and fingers (although the finger data is not used in this paper) totaling 96 markers, which were post-processed to compute the human body kinematic models by extracting the accurate joint center coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-129: The dataset contains 2D and 3D ground truth and the corresponding audio.
elements.sent-2411-130: To highlight the need for a multimodal, diverse, fully calibrated music dataset, a comparison to generic motion datasets, dance datasets, and music datasets is presented in Table \ref{tab:dataset_comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-131: We used the Mean Per Joint Position Error (MPJPE), MPJPE after Procrustes analysis (P-MPJPE), Mean Per Joint Velocity Error (MPJVE), Mean Per Joint Acceleration Error (MPJAE), and Dynamic Time Warping (DTW).
elements.sent-2411-133: DTW and MPJAE measure the similarity between the generated trajectory and the ground truth trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-134: We found that subtle movements, such as the vibrato, present a challenge for accurate motion estimation.
elements.sent-2411-135: Thus, a model that only estimates the average trajectory position may yield a low MPJPE but a high MPJVE, MPJAE, and DTW.
elements.sent-2411-136: Therefore, we need to consider all the metrics to understand the model's fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-137: We benchmarked our results against other state-of-the-art architectures on \DataName.
elements.sent-2411-138: To ensure a fair comparison, we retrained these architectures using our dataset.
elements.sent-2411-140: We established the hip position (calculated as the average value of the pelvis) as a reference.
elements.sent-2411-141: Still, our algorithm, \AlgName, demonstrated superior performance; compared to the best values of others, we achieved a 5.40 \% improvement in MPJPE.
elements.sent-2411-142: Remarkably, \AlgName \ achieved a significant 17.37 \% and 39.64 \% improvement in MPJVE and MPJAE values, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-143: Furthermore, \AlgName \ without the audio model also achieved the second best performance in all the metrics except P-MPJPE.
elements.sent-2411-144: Unlike other datasets, \DataName \ contains extreme motion disparity; the lower body limbs mostly stay still but the upper body limbs dynamically move.
elements.sent-2411-145: Because of these special conditions, it is preferable to evaluate joint-wise position accuracy rather than MPJPE, see Table~\ref{tab:result_human3d_pose_joints .}
elements.sent-2411-146: Based on the result, we observed that the most accuracy gains came from estimating accurate upper body limbs.
elements.sent-2411-147: For example, we achieved a maximum 7.82 \% accuracy gain in left index estimation and 8.57 \% accuracy gain in right wrist estimation compared to other best SoTA results.
elements.sent-2411-148: These results demonstrate that our proposed architecture successfully estimates the complex motion dynamics of violin playing.
elements.sent-2411-149: We also illustrate the right and left wrist trajectories in Fig. ~\ref{fig:trajectory and Fig.~\ref{fig:vibrato , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-150: Furthermore, we demonstrate a qualitative full-body estimation result in Fig.~\ref{fig:qualitative_rsult .}
elements.sent-2411-151: In Fig.~\ref{fig:trajectory , we highlight the articulation of the estimated right-hand wrist trajectories compared to other SoTA methods.}
elements.sent-2411-152: For example, most other SoTA methods decently estimate large and straightforward motions (first column of the figure) but tend to fail to estimate small (second column of the figure) and complex motions (third and last column of the figure).
elements.sent-2411-153: This is mainly because they achieve good MPJPE value even though they ignore fine grained motions.
elements.sent-2411-154: However, \AlgName \ explicitly learns motion dynamics via the hierarchical structure ($\S$ ~\ref{sec:hierarchy ) and bidirectional mixing module ($\S$ ~\ref{sec:mixing ), guiding the model to track sophisticated motions as well.}}
elements.sent-2411-155: These phenomena are well observed for the case of vibrato in Fig. ~\ref{fig:vibrato .}
elements.sent-2411-156: Unlike bowing motion, vibrato lasts only a few seconds and has very fast and tiny motions (approximately 10 mm perturbation).
elements.sent-2411-157: Therefore, other SoTA methods tend to estimate simple straight lines rather than subtle perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-158: Note that, even though they estimate straight lines, they still achieve approximately 10 mm error, which is less than the average MPJPE value.
elements.sent-2411-159: However, to gain a better understanding of human motion in musical performance, it is crucial to estimate those sophisticated motion dynamics as well.
elements.sent-2411-160: Especially in such cases, audio plays a significant role in compensating information, compared to \AlgName \ and \AlgName \ w/o Audio in Fig.~\ref{fig:vibrato .}
elements.figure-2411-009: \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l | c|c|c|c|c|c} \Xhline{1pt} Method CNN MPJPE $\downarrow$ P-MPJPE $\downarrow$ MPJVE $\downarrow$ MPJAE $\downarrow$ DTW $\downarrow$ Spectrogram (100 SR) 1D 44.01 28.01 1.56 1.03 6.70 30 SR 1D 44.11 27.39 1.55 1.01 6.74 100 SR (Baseline) 1D 43.60 27.77 1.57 1.02 6.67 100 SR 2D 45.01 27.98 1.64 1.08 6.83 100 SR w/o 45.39 28.69 1.62 1.08 6.84 300 SR 1D 43.51 27.86 1.65 1.10 6.69 \Xhline{1pt} \end{tabular} } \caption{Ablation study of different audio inputs: spectrogram (100 SR), 30, 100, 300 sampling rate audio features, 1D CNN, 2D CNN, and without CNN.} \label{tab:abl_audio}
elements.sent-2411-161: We conducted four ablation studies; specifically, we evaluated the audio module, the hierarchy module, the bidirectional mixing module, and different input frame sizes.
elements.sent-2411-162: We explored different types of audio inputs, sampling rates, and CNN models, as shown in Table ~\ref{tab:abl_audio .}
elements.sent-2411-163: We experimented with sampling rates ranging from 30 to 300 to assess their impacts.
elements.sent-2411-164: We also tested spectrogram (100 SR) inputs, standard in the audiovisual learning literature~\cite{suPhysicsDrivenDiffusionModels2023, suAudeoAudioGeneration2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-165: In terms of metrics, 1D CNNs demonstrated superior results over 2D CNNs and basic MLPs (see w/o CNN experiment in Table~\ref{tab:abl_audio ) for computing latent vector spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-166: Increasing the sampling rate, the model showed better results in MPJPE.
elements.sent-2411-167: In conclusion, the key to performance improvement is using the mixture of audio features~\cite{liAIChoreographerMusic2021, marchellusM2CConciseMusic2023 with a 1D CNN feature extractor and a higher sampling rate.}
elements.figure-2411-010: \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l| c| c| c | c| c } \Xhline{1pt} Method MPJPE $\downarrow$ P-MPJPE $\downarrow$ MPJVE $\downarrow$ MPJAE $\downarrow$ DTW $\downarrow$ \AlgName \ (Baseline) 43.60 27.77 1.57 1.02 6.67 w/o Cascade 44.21 28.15 1.62 1.07 6.73 Concatenation 43.95 27.95 1.56 1.02 6.72 Conditioning 44.18 29.01 1.90 1.42 6.75 Parallel 45.17 28.40 2.19 2.42 6.81 w/o Int. 44.75 28.44 1.57 1.04 6.78 w/o Diff. 46.02 27.99 1.90 1.50 6.92 w/o Mixing 47.87 29.61 2.00 1.34 7.10 \Xhline{1pt} \end{tabular} } \caption{Ablation study on the efficacy of the modules in \AlgName. Bold numbers represent the best values.} \label{tab:abl}
elements.sent-2411-168: We evaluated the efficacy of the hierarchy module (detailed in $\S$~\ref{sec:hierarchy ), as shown in Table ~\ref{tab:abl .}}
elements.sent-2411-169: Our ablation study includes three scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-170: First, we examined a model without Eq.~\eqref{eq:sum , referred to as w/o Cascade in the table.}
elements.sent-2411-171: Second, we replaced Eq.~\eqref{eq:sum with a concatenation operator ($\|$): \begin{equation}h_{i}^{(n)}= Norm(g_{i}^{(n)}\| h_{i+1}^{(n)})\end{equation} Lastly, we removed Eq.~\eqref{eq:sum and replaced Eq.~\eqref{eq:hierarchy with a cross-transformer.}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: Formally, \begin{equation}h_{i}^{(n)}=Norm(CrossTransformer(h_{i}^{(n-1)}, h_{i+1}^{(n)}))\end{equation} where $\text{CrossTransformer(x,y)}$ follows a computation similar as in Eq.~\eqref{eq:transformer , but in the multi-head attention computation, $x$ becomes query and $y$ becomes key and value~\cite{attention .}}
elements.sent-2411-173: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:abl , the w/o Cascade model shows the worst result in MPJPE.}
elements.sent-2411-174: However, overall the performance drops are minor.
elements.sent-2411-175: This is mainly because the architecture maintains the hierarchy structure and explicitly exchanges information through the bidirectional mixing module.
elements.sent-2411-176: Once we remove the top-down integrated estimation (Eq. ~\eqref{eq:vel_int and Eq.~\eqref{eq:pose_int ) and bottom-up differentiated estimation (Eq.~\eqref{eq:vel_diff and Eq.~\eqref{eq:acc_diff ), the architecture becomes parallel and the performance drops significantly, especially in MPJVE and MPJAE values (Parallel in Table~\ref{tab:abl ).}}}}}

Bold numbers represent the best values.} \label{tab:abl_frame}
elements.sent-2411-177: We evaluated the efficacy of the bidirectional mixing module (\S ~\ref{sec:mixing ) as shown in Table ~\ref{tab:abl .}}
elements.sent-2411-178: First, we removed Eq.~\eqref{eq:vel_int and Eq.~\eqref{eq:pose_int , referred to as w/o Int..}}
elements.sent-2411-179: Second, we removed Eq.~\eqref{eq:vel_diff and Eq.~\eqref{eq:acc_diff , referred to as w/o Diff..}}

elements.sent-2411-181: Based on the results, bottom-up motion dynamics mixing is important.
elements.sent-2411-182: We assume this is because the architecture already exchanges top-down information through the hierarchy module.

We computed the mean value and standard deviation over trials. \textcolor{red}{Red} and \textcolor{blue}{blue} represent first and second best, respectively.} \label{tab:result_performance_wild}
elements.sent-2411-188: We evaluated four tasks to showcase the algorithm's usefulness for violin performance analysis.
elements.sent-2411-189: First, we used it for segmenting bowing direction change, essential for other downstream tasks related to bow stroke analysis.
elements.sent-2411-190: We low-pass filtered the right wrist estimates and computed the local minima of the acceleration to locate the bowing direction change.
elements.sent-2411-191: Comparison with ground truth is within a temporal window of acceptance.
elements.sent-2411-192: Second, we monitored the bowing trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-193: Ideally, the bow is parallel to the bridge (we call this a straight bow in Table \ref{tab:result_performance_wild ).}
elements.sent-2411-194: For each segmented bow stroke, we measured the correctness of the curvature of the right wrist trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-195: Thirdly, we monitored the left wrist flexion (useful for evaluating violin hold) from the hand, wrist, and elbow positions and evaluated using the $L_1$ distance to the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-197: We computed local minima of acceleration of the mid-point of the left wrist, pinkie, and index finger, to obtain location direction change and computed temporal alignment with the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-198: For segmenting bowing direction change, \AlgName\ is 3.1\% to 31.2\% more accurate than the SoTA and 3.1\% better than without audio.
elements.sent-2411-199: For the straight bow analysis, \AlgName\ was 15.3\% to 95.0\% more accurate than the SoTA and 23.5\% more accurate than without the audio model.
elements.sent-2411-200: For violin hold, \AlgName \ is 4.0\% to 57.8\% more accurate and 5.9\% improvement with audio.
elements.sent-2411-201: And finally, for vibrato, \AlgName \ is 10.2\% to 17.8\% more accurate than SoTA and 17.5\% improvement over the non-audio model.
elements.sent-2411-202: These results clearly demonstrate why a more accurate and stable 3D pose estimation is needed and across the board audio contributes to much better model prediction.
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/keypoints.png} \caption{ Qualitative results, tested on \DataName \ P08C01T28 with 10 frames interval (30 FPS). Green and blue solid keypoints and are ground truth 3D pose and prediction, respectively. Red circles highlight incorrect estimations. } \label{fig:qualitative_rsult}
elements.sent-2411-203: We introduced a large violin dataset, captured with four cameras and paired microphones and synchronized and calibrated with the 2D and 3D poses.
elements.sent-2411-204: It features players of diverse genders, ages, sizes, and expertise who play common practice materials and complex repertoires.
elements.sent-2411-205: We demonstrated the shortfalls of existing SoTA pose estimation algorithms for violin playing.
elements.sent-2411-206: We then presented an audiovisual monocular 4D pose estimation network with a novel hierarchical structure outperforming current SoTA methods, and demonstrated the usefulness of the method for violin performance analysis.
elements.sent-2411-207: In the next step in our future work, we plan to analyze and segment the video into elementary segments \cite{guerra2005discovering,li2010learning , which are meaningful to music performance.}
elements.sent-2411-208: We greatly acknowledge NSF's support under awards OISE 2020624 and BCS 2318255 and UMD's Grand Challenge Grant for "Music Education for All Through Personalized AI and Digital Humanities".. { \bibliographystyle{IEEEtranSN.}}
elements.sent-2411-209: If you are submitting a paper to the second round for a paper that received a Revise decision in the first round, please use this template to address the reviewer concerns and highlight the changes made since the original submission.
elements.sent-2411-210: The rebuttal is limited to a {\bf one page PDF file.}
elements.sent-2411-211: Please follow the steps and style guidelines outlined below for submitting your author response.
elements.sent-2411-212: Just like the original submission, the rebuttal must maintain anonymity and cannot include external links that reveal the author identity or circumvent the length restriction.
elements.sent-2411-213: The rebuttal must comply with this template (the use of sections is not required, though it is recommended to structure the rebuttal for ease of reading).
elements.sent-2411-214: Author responses must be no longer than 1 page in length including any references and figures.
elements.sent-2411-215: Overlength responses will simply not be reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-216: This includes responses where the margins and formatting are deemed to have been significantly altered from those laid down by this style guide.
elements.sent-2411-217: Note that this \LaTeX\ guide already sets figure captions and references in a smaller font.
elements.sent-2411-218: All text must be in a two-column format.
elements.sent-2411-219: The total allowable size of the text area is $6\frac78$ inches (17.46 cm) wide by $8\frac78$ inches (22.54 cm) high.
elements.sent-2411-220: Columns are to be $3\frac14$ inches (8.25 cm) wide, with a $\frac{5 {16}$ inch (0.8 cm) space between them.}
elements.sent-2411-221: The top margin should begin 1 inch (2.54 cm) from the top edge of the page.
elements.sent-2411-222: The bottom margin should be $1\frac{1 {8}$ inches (2.86 cm) from the bottom edge of the page for $8.5 \times 11$-inch paper; for A4 paper, approximately $1\frac{5 {8}$ inches (4.13 cm) from the bottom edge of the page.}}
elements.sent-2411-224: It is important for readers to be able to refer to any particular equation.
elements.sent-2411-225: Wherever Times is specified, Times Roman may also be used.
elements.sent-2411-226: Main text should be in 10-point Times, single-spaced.
elements.sent-2411-227: Section headings should be in 10 or 12 point Times.
elements.sent-2411-228: All paragraphs should be indented 1 pica (approx.~$\frac{1 {6}$ inch or 0.422 cm).}
elements.sent-2411-229: Figure and table captions should be 9-point Roman type as in Figure \ref{fig:onecol .}
elements.sent-2411-230: List and number all bibliographical references in 9-point Times, single-spaced, at the end of your response.
elements.sent-2411-231: When referenced in the text, enclose the citation number in square brackets, for example~\cite{Alpher05 .}
elements.sent-2411-232: Where appropriate, include the name(s) of editors of referenced books.
elements.figure-2411-014: \fbox{\rule{0pt}{0.5in} \rule{0.9\linewidth}{0pt}} \caption{Example of caption. It is set in Roman so that mathematics (always set in Roman: $B \sin A = A \sin B$) may be included without an ugly clash.} \label{fig:onecol}
elements.sent-2411-233: To avoid ambiguities, it is best if the numbering for equations, figures, tables, and references in the author response does not overlap with that in the main paper (the reviewer may wonder if you talk about Figure \ref{fig:onecol in the author response or in the paper).}
elements.sent-2411-236: Please ensure that any point you wish to make is resolvable in a printed copy of the response.
